subject: ap-precalculus
title: Polynomial Modeling: Covariation, Finite Differences & Concentration Analysis
stimulus:
After a patient takes a medication, the concentration of the drug in the bloodstream (in micrograms per milliliter) can be modeled by a polynomial function $C$ for $0 \leq t \leq 9$, where $t$ is time in hours after taking the medication. Selected values of $C(t)$ are given in the table below.

| $t$ (hours) | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $4$ | $5$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C(t)$ ($\mu$g/mL) | $0.5$ | $3.5$ | $12.5$ | $25.1$ | $38.9$ | $51.5$ |

parts.prompt:
(i) Compute the average rates of change of $C$ over the intervals $[0, 1]$, $[1, 2]$, $[2, 3]$, and $[3, 4]$. Show the computations that lead to your answer.

(ii) Based on the first differences (average rates of change over unit intervals) computed in part (i), determine whether the function $C$ is better modeled by a linear function or a higher-degree polynomial on the interval $0 \leq t \leq 4$. Justify your answer by referring to what the first differences would look like if $C$ were linear.
parts.scoring_notes: The four differences (over unit intervals) are: $[0,1]$: $3.0$; $[1,2]$: $9.0$; $[2,3]$: $12.6$; $[3,4]$: $13.8$. If $C$ were linear, all first differences would be equal (constant). Since $3.0, 9.0, 12.6, 13.8$ are not constant, $C$ is not linear — a higher-degree polynomial is appropriate. The justification must explicitly state that linear functions have constant first differences.

scoring_points.description: All four average rates of change correct: $3.0$, $9.0$, $12.6$, $13.8$; supporting work must show each quotient
wrong_examples: Computing differences $3.5 - 0.5 = 3.0$ but not dividing by the interval length (though here $\Delta t = 1$ so they are the same)
common_traps: Since all intervals have length $1$, the average rate of change equals the simple difference in $C$ values — but showing the quotient form is good practice

scoring_points.description: Correct conclusion (higher-degree polynomial) with justification that a linear function would have constant first differences, but $3.0, 9.0, 12.6, 13.8$ are not constant

required_elements: linear functions have constant first differences
alternatives.correct_example: If $C$ were linear, the first differences would all be equal. The first differences $3.0, 9.0, 12.6, 13.8$ are not constant, so $C$ is not linear on $[0, 4]$ and a higher-degree polynomial model is more appropriate.

wrong_examples: Concluding 'linear' — incorrect, the first differences are not constant
Saying 'not linear' without explaining what constant first differences would look like
common_traps: The justification must explicitly reference what constant first differences would mean — not just that the values change

parts.prompt:
(i) Compute the second differences of $C$ (the differences of consecutive first differences from part (a)). Based on the second differences, determine whether a quadratic polynomial is an appropriate model for $C$ on $0 \leq t \leq 4$. Justify your answer.

(ii) Find the average rate of change of $C$ from $t = 1$ to $t = 5$ hours. Express your answer with appropriate units, and describe in context what this value represents about the drug concentration.
parts.scoring_notes: Second differences: $9.0 - 3.0 = 6.0$; $12.6 - 9.0 = 3.6$; $13.8 - 12.6 = 1.2$. Not constant, so quadratic is not appropriate. For part (ii), $\frac{51.5 - 3.5}{5 - 1} = \frac{48.0}{4} = 12.0$ $\mu$g/mL/hr. Contextual description requires all three: direction (increasing), rate ($12.0$), and time interval ($t = 1$ to $t = 5$ hours). Units required.

scoring_points.description: Correct second differences $6.0, 3.6, 1.2$ and conclusion that a quadratic model is NOT appropriate because a quadratic would produce constant second differences

required_elements: quadratic functions have constant second differences
second differences are not constant — quadratic not appropriate
alternatives.correct_example: Second differences: $9.0 - 3.0 = 6.0$; $12.6 - 9.0 = 3.6$; $13.8 - 12.6 = 1.2$. These are not constant. A quadratic function would produce constant second differences, so a quadratic model is not appropriate — a cubic or higher-degree polynomial is needed.

wrong_examples: Concluding 'quadratic is appropriate' — incorrect, second differences are not constant
common_traps: Quadratic functions have CONSTANT second differences — if second differences vary, the degree must be higher than 2

scoring_points.description: Correct average rate of change $12.0$ $\mu$g/mL per hour with all three contextual components: increasing direction, rate value, and time interval

alternatives.correct_example: $\frac{C(5) - C(1)}{5 - 1} = \frac{51.5 - 3.5}{4} = 12.0$ micrograms per milliliter per hour. On average, the drug concentration in the bloodstream increased by $12.0$ $\mu$g/mL each hour between $1$ and $5$ hours after the medication was taken.

common_traps: All three components required: (1) direction (increasing), (2) rate ($12.0$ $\mu$g/mL/hr), and (3) time interval ($t = 1$ to $5$ hours)

parts.prompt:
A doctor determines the medication is effective only when the concentration exceeds $20$ micrograms per milliliter. The complete model is given by $C(t) = -0.4t^3 + 4.2t^2 - 0.8t + 0.5$ for $0 \leq t \leq 9$.

(i) Use technology to find all times $t$ in $0 \leq t \leq 9$ at which $C(t) = 20$. Express the values as decimal approximations to the nearest hundredth.

(ii) State the time interval(s) during which the medication is effective. Then find the value of $C(9)$ and interpret its meaning in the context of this problem.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), solving $C(t) = 20$ in $[0, 9]$: $C(9) = -0.4(729) + 4.2(81) - 7.2 + 0.5 = -291.6 + 340.2 - 7.2 + 0.5 = 41.9 > 20$. Only one real root in $[0, 9]$ near $t \approx 2.62$ (accept $[2.58, 2.66]$). For part (ii), effective interval is approximately $[2.62, 9]$. $C(9) \approx 41.9$ $\mu$g/mL — still above $20$ at $t = 9$. Contextual interpretation must reference the $20$ $\mu$g/mL threshold.

scoring_points.description: Correct time $t \approx 2.62$ (accept $[2.58, 2.66]$) from technology; note $C(9) > 20$ so only one crossing in $[0, 9]$

alternatives.correct_example: Using technology, $C(t) = 20$ has one solution in $[0, 9]$ at $t \approx 2.62$. Since $C(9) \approx 41.9 > 20$, the concentration does not drop below $20$ again within the modeled domain.

wrong_examples: Giving two solutions (assuming the curve crosses $20$ twice when it only crosses once)
Using algebra instead of technology for a cubic equation
common_traps: The question specifies technology — verify by evaluating $C(9)$ to confirm concentration stays above $20$ through $t = 9$

scoring_points.description: Effective interval $[2.62, 9]$ (or consistent with student's $t$-value from part (i)); AND $C(9) \approx 41.9$ $\mu$g/mL with interpretation that the medication is still effective at the end of the modeled period

required_elements: interpretation: medication is still effective at $t = 9$
alternatives.correct_example: The medication is effective on approximately $[2.62, 9]$ hours. $C(9) = -0.4(729) + 4.2(81) - 7.2 + 0.5 \approx 41.9$ $\mu$g/mL $> 20$, meaning the concentration remains above the effective threshold at the end of the modeled period.

wrong_examples: Effective interval listed as $(0, 9]$ (incorrectly including the interval before $t \approx 2.62$)
common_traps: The interval begins at the crossing time $\approx 2.62$, not at $t = 0$ — the drug is not immediately effective
